does: This paper introduces a comprehensive framework for improving video captioning through structured visual specifications and human-AI collaboration. The authors develop a detailed taxonomy of visual primitives covering subjects, scenes, motion, spatial relationships, and camera dynamics, created in consultation with professional filmmakers. They propose CHAI (Critique-based Human-AI Oversight), a workflow where trained experts critique and refine model-generated captions rather than annotating from scratch. The resulting datasets and preference signals enable training improvements to open-source video language models (Qwen3-VL) using supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and inference-time scaling. The approach demonstrates that delegating text generation to models while focusing human effort on verification yields both higher-quality captions and more efficient annotation.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research in video understanding and video-language modeling, extending prior work on dense video captioning and video-text alignment. It responds to limitations in existing video datasets and annotation methods by adopting ideas from human-in-the-loop machine learning and preference learning frameworks like DPO that have shown success in text generation. The structured specification approach draws on work in formal video semantics and shot annotation from film studies. The oversight methodology connects to broader research on scalable supervision and critique-based learning, where human feedback focuses on refinement rather than generation from scratch. The paper synthesizes these threads with recent advances in large vision-language models to address the specific challenge of precise video understanding at scale.
enables: This work opens several directions for future research in video understanding and multimodal learning. The structured visual primitives and annotation framework could support more precise evaluation of video-language models beyond standard benchmarks, enabling detailed diagnostic testing of model capabilities. The CHAI framework provides a template for scalable oversight applicable to other dense video understanding tasks like action detection, scene understanding, or fine-grained temporal localization. The preference data and critique generation capabilities could advance research in interpretability and model alignment for vision tasks. Additionally, the approach suggests methods for scaling video annotation to much larger datasets while maintaining quality, potentially enabling training of significantly more capable video-language models. The open datasets and recipes promise to accelerate community research on video understanding and fair evaluation of multimodal systems.
